title: What Is ARV (After Repair Value)? Definition & How to Calculate | VA Horizon
description: ARV (After Repair Value) is the estimated market value of a property after renovations. Learn how wholesalers calculate ARV from comps and use it to size offers.
shortDefinition: After Repair Value (ARV) is the estimated market value a property will be worth once it has been fully repaired and renovated. It is the anchor number wholesalers and investors use to back into a maximum offer.
longExplanation:
ARV is the foundation of almost every wholesale deal. Before you can decide what to offer a seller, you need a defensible estimate of what the finished, renovated home would sell for on the open market. That number is the ARV.

Wholesalers establish ARV by pulling recent comparable sales (comps): similar homes in the same area, ideally sold in the last 90 to 180 days, with similar square footage, bed/bath count, and condition. You average the price per square foot of the best comps and apply it to the subject property in its renovated state. A reliable ARV protects both you and your cash buyer, because the entire deal is priced as a percentage of it.
formula: Offer math: ARV × 0.70 − Repairs − Your Fee = Max Offer
example: A 1,400 sq ft house needs a full rehab. Three renovated comps nearby sold around $200 per sq ft, so the ARV is roughly 1,400 × $200 = $280,000. With $40,000 in repairs and a $10,000 wholesale fee, a 70% rule offer would be 280,000 × 0.70 − 40,000 − 10,000 = $146,000.

faq.q: How accurate does my ARV need to be?
faq.a: Accurate enough that a cash buyer agrees with it. If your ARV is inflated, buyers will renegotiate or walk, and your deal falls apart. Pull conservative comps and, when possible, confirm your number with an active buyer before you lock up the contract.

faq.q: Is ARV the same as the current value?
faq.a: No. The current (as-is) value reflects the home in its present, often distressed, condition. ARV is the projected value after renovations are complete. The spread between the two is where wholesale and flip profit lives.

title: What Is MAO (Maximum Allowable Offer)? Formula & Example | VA Horizon
description: MAO (Maximum Allowable Offer) is the highest price a wholesaler can pay and still profit. Learn the 70% rule formula and how to calculate your MAO.
shortDefinition: Maximum Allowable Offer (MAO) is the highest price a wholesaler or investor can offer on a property and still leave enough margin for repairs, the end buyer's profit, and their own fee.
longExplanation:
MAO keeps you disciplined. It is the ceiling on what you can pay a seller, calculated backward from the ARV so that every party in the deal still makes money. Going above your MAO is how wholesalers end up with contracts no cash buyer will take.

The most common starting point is the 70% rule: take 70% of ARV, then subtract estimated repairs and your wholesale fee. The 70% leaves room for your buyer's profit and holding costs. In hotter markets investors sometimes flex to 75% or higher, and in slower markets they tighten it. Whatever percentage you use, the MAO is the number you must not exceed when negotiating.
example: On a property with a $280,000 ARV, $40,000 in repairs, and a target $10,000 fee: MAO = (280,000 × 0.70) − 40,000 − 10,000 = $146,000. That is the most you should offer the seller.

faq.q: Why 70%?
faq.a: The 70% rule is a convention that leaves roughly 30% of ARV to cover the end buyer's profit, holding costs, and closing costs. It is a starting point, not a law: adjust the percentage based on your market and your buyer's expectations.

faq.q: Should I open negotiations at my MAO?
faq.a: No. Your MAO is the maximum, not the opening offer. Start below it so you have room to negotiate up while staying inside a profitable range.

title: What Are Comps (Comparable Sales) in Real Estate? | VA Horizon
description: Comps are recently sold properties similar to the one you are valuing. Learn how wholesalers pull comps to estimate ARV and price offers accurately.
shortDefinition: Comparable sales, or comps, are recently sold properties similar to the subject property in location, size, age, and condition. Wholesalers use comps to establish a defensible ARV.
longExplanation:
Comps turn valuation from a guess into evidence. To estimate what a renovated property will sell for, you find homes that already sold nearby and were genuinely comparable, then use their sale prices to project the subject's value.

Good comps share the subject's neighborhood, are within roughly 20% of its square footage, match bed and bath count where possible, and sold recently, usually within the last 90 to 180 days. You generally lean on sold prices rather than active listings, because a listing only shows what a seller hopes to get, not what a buyer actually paid.
example: To value a 3-bed, 2-bath, 1,500 sq ft home, you pull three renovated 3/2 homes within a half mile that sold in the last four months for $285,000, $292,000, and $279,000. Their average supports an ARV near $285,000.

faq.q: How many comps do I need?
faq.a: Three to five strong, recent, nearby sold comps is a solid baseline. Quality matters more than quantity: one clean comparable beats five distant or outdated ones.

faq.q: Can I use active listings as comps?
faq.a: Use them with caution. Active listings show asking prices, not sale prices, so they tell you about competition and ceiling expectations rather than true market value. Always anchor your ARV to sold comps.

title: What Is an Assignment of Contract in Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: An assignment of contract lets a wholesaler transfer their purchase agreement to an end buyer for a fee. Learn how assignment works and when to use it.
shortDefinition: An assignment of contract is the most common wholesale exit: the wholesaler signs a purchase agreement with a seller, then transfers (assigns) their rights in that contract to an end buyer for an assignment fee.
longExplanation:
In a standard assignment, the wholesaler never actually buys the property. They put it under contract, securing the right to purchase it, and then sell that right to a cash buyer who closes in their place. The wholesaler's profit is the assignment fee, paid at closing.

This works because a real estate purchase contract is itself an asset with value. Wholesalers typically add an 'and/or assigns' clause to the buyer line so the agreement is clearly assignable. Assignment is fast and capital-light, but it requires that your contract permits assignment and that local rules allow it. Some states and some seller situations (such as bank-owned deals) restrict assignment, which is when a double close is used instead.
example: You contract a house for $146,000 and assign that contract to a cash buyer for $156,000. At closing, the buyer pays $146,000 to the seller and a $10,000 assignment fee to you. You never took title.

faq.q: Is an assignment fee disclosed to the seller?
faq.a: Best practice, and in many states a legal requirement, is to disclose that you intend to assign the contract and that you will earn a fee. Transparency protects the deal and keeps you compliant. Always confirm your state's rules with a real estate attorney.

faq.q: When can't I assign a contract?
faq.a: When the contract prohibits assignment, when the lender or seller (often banks or some institutional sellers) bars it, or when state rules require a different structure. In those cases wholesalers typically use a double close.

title: What Is a Double Close in Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: A double close is two back-to-back transactions where a wholesaler briefly buys then resells a property. Learn when to double close instead of assigning.
shortDefinition: A double close (or double closing) is when a wholesaler actually buys the property and then resells it to the end buyer in two separate, often same-day, transactions, keeping the spread between the two prices.
longExplanation:
A double close is the alternative to an assignment when assigning is not allowed or not desirable. Instead of transferring a contract, the wholesaler completes a genuine A-to-B purchase from the seller, then immediately completes a B-to-C sale to the cash buyer.

Wholesalers choose a double close to keep their fee private (the seller and end buyer never see each other's price), or when a contract, lender, or state rule blocks assignment. The trade-off is cost and complexity: there are two sets of closing costs, and you may need transactional funding to cover the first purchase for the brief window before the second closing funds.
example: You buy a property from the seller for $146,000 (the A-to-B close), then resell it to your cash buyer for $158,000 (the B-to-C close) the same day. Your $12,000 spread stays between the two closings and is not disclosed across the table.

faq.q: Do I need my own money to double close?
faq.a: Often you use transactional (same-day) funding that covers the A-to-B purchase for the hours between the two closings, repaid immediately when the B-to-C sale funds. Some wholesalers use their own capital, but most use short-term transactional lenders.

faq.q: Why double close instead of assign?
faq.a: To keep your spread confidential, or because the contract, the lender, or your state's rules do not allow a simple assignment. The cost is two sets of closing fees, so wholesalers usually assign when they can and double close when they must.

title: What Is a Wholesale Fee (Assignment Fee)? | VA Horizon
description: The wholesale fee is the profit a wholesaler earns on a deal, paid by the end buyer at closing. Learn how assignment fees are set and what is typical.
shortDefinition: The wholesale fee, also called the assignment fee, is the wholesaler's profit on a deal: the difference between the price under contract with the seller and the price the end buyer pays, collected at closing.
longExplanation:
The wholesale fee is how wholesalers get paid. When you assign a contract, the fee is the markup between your contract price with the seller and the amount your cash buyer agrees to pay. In a double close, it is the spread between the A-to-B and B-to-C prices.

Fee size depends on the deal's spread, the market, and how much value you added in finding and negotiating the deal. Many wholesalers target fees in the $5,000 to $15,000 range, though larger spreads on bigger deals can produce more. The fee must fit inside the MAO math, because if it pushes the end buyer's all-in cost too high, the buyer walks.
example: You lock a property at $146,000 and assign it to a buyer for $156,000. The $10,000 difference is your wholesale fee, paid to you by the title company at closing.

faq.q: What is a typical wholesale fee?
faq.a: Many deals land between $5,000 and $15,000, but there is no fixed standard. The fee is whatever the spread supports while still leaving the end buyer a profitable purchase. Bigger spreads on higher-value properties can justify larger fees.

faq.q: Who pays the wholesale fee?
faq.a: The end buyer effectively pays it: it is built into the price they agree to, and the title company disburses it to you at closing. The seller receives their agreed contract price.

title: What Is an Earnest Money Deposit (EMD) in Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: An earnest money deposit (EMD) is a good-faith payment that secures a purchase contract. Learn how EMD works in wholesale deals and how to limit risk.
shortDefinition: An earnest money deposit (EMD) is a good-faith payment a buyer puts down when signing a purchase contract, showing the seller the offer is serious. It is typically held in escrow and applied at closing.
longExplanation:
EMD signals commitment. When a wholesaler puts a property under contract, the seller often expects an earnest money deposit held by the title company or attorney. If the deal closes, the EMD is credited toward the purchase. If the buyer backs out for a reason not protected by the contract, the seller may keep it.

Wholesalers manage EMD risk in a few ways: negotiating a small deposit (sometimes as low as $100 to $1,000 on distressed deals), using an inspection or due-diligence contingency that allows a refund within a set window, and lining up the end buyer quickly so the deal is assigned before the deposit is truly at risk.
example: You sign a contract with a $500 EMD and a 10-day inspection contingency. You assign the deal to a cash buyer on day 6. The contingency window protected your $500, and the buyer closes in your place.

faq.q: How much earnest money should a wholesaler put down?
faq.a: As little as the seller will accept while still treating the offer as credible. On distressed deals, $100 to $1,000 is common. The goal is to keep your risk small while you secure an end buyer.

faq.q: Can I get my EMD back?
faq.a: Usually yes if you cancel within a contingency window written into the contract, such as an inspection or due-diligence period. Outside those protections, the seller may be entitled to keep it, so contingencies matter.

title: What Is a Motivated Seller in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: A motivated seller needs to sell quickly due to a pressing situation. Learn what creates motivation and how wholesalers find and qualify these sellers.
shortDefinition: A motivated seller is a property owner with a pressing reason to sell quickly, often for less than full market price. Finding and qualifying motivated sellers is the core of wholesale lead generation.
longExplanation:
Motivation is what makes a wholesale deal possible. Owners who are not motivated will hold out for full retail value, which leaves no room for a wholesale spread. Motivated sellers, by contrast, value speed, certainty, and convenience over squeezing out the last dollar.

Common drivers of motivation include foreclosure, divorce, probate or inherited property, tired landlords, costly repairs, job relocation, and tax or code issues. Wholesalers reach these sellers through cold calling, SMS, direct mail, and driving for dollars, then qualify them on a few key points: their situation, timeline, asking price, and the property's condition.
example: An out-of-state owner inherits a house that needs $50,000 of work. They have no interest in managing a renovation or a listing, so they accept a fast cash offer below retail. That motivation is what creates the spread.

faq.q: How do wholesalers find motivated sellers?
faq.a: Through outbound channels: cold calling and SMS to targeted lists (absentee owners, high-equity, pre-foreclosure), direct mail, and driving for dollars to spot distressed properties. High call and message volume on a good list is what surfaces motivated sellers consistently.

faq.q: How do you qualify a motivated seller?
faq.a: Ask about their situation and why they are selling, their timeline, their price expectation, and the property's condition. Those four answers tell you whether there is a workable deal and how to structure your offer.

title: What Is Skip Tracing in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Skip tracing is the process of finding a property owner's current phone number and contact info. Learn how wholesalers use skip tracing to reach sellers.
shortDefinition: Skip tracing is the process of finding accurate, current contact information, especially phone numbers, for a property owner so a wholesaler can actually reach them to discuss buying their property.
longExplanation:
A list of properties is useless if you cannot reach the owners. Skip tracing bridges that gap. Starting from an owner's name and property address, skip tracing services return likely phone numbers, mailing addresses, and sometimes email addresses by matching against public records, utility data, and other databases.

Wholesalers skip trace lists of absentee owners, high-equity owners, or pre-foreclosure owners before a cold calling or SMS campaign. Match rates and number quality vary by provider, so experienced operators treat skip-traced data as a starting point and let the dialer and callers quickly weed out wrong numbers. Better data means more live connections per dial and a more productive campaign.
example: You build a list of 5,000 absentee-owner properties but have only addresses. You run them through a skip-tracing service, which returns one or more phone numbers per owner, and load those numbers into the dialer for your cold callers.

faq.q: Do VA Horizon's cold callers skip trace?
faq.a: No. The cold caller's job is narrowly defined: call, qualify, and submit leads. VA Horizon handles list sourcing and skip-tracing coordination on the back end so the caller can focus entirely on conversations.

faq.q: Why do skip-traced numbers sometimes fail?
faq.a: Public-record and database matching is probabilistic, so some returned numbers are outdated or belong to the wrong person. A predictive dialer and trained callers clear bad numbers quickly, which is why volume and data quality both matter.

title: What Is Driving for Dollars in Real Estate? | VA Horizon
description: Driving for dollars means driving neighborhoods to spot distressed properties and build a targeted seller list. Learn how wholesalers use this lead source.
shortDefinition: Driving for dollars is the practice of physically driving through neighborhoods to spot distressed or neglected properties, recording their addresses, and turning them into a targeted list of potential motivated sellers.
longExplanation:
Driving for dollars is one of the oldest and lowest-cost lead sources in wholesaling. The logic is simple: a property that looks neglected (overgrown yard, boarded windows, code violations, deferred maintenance) often signals an owner who is absentee, financially stretched, or otherwise motivated.

Wholesalers note these addresses, then look up the owners and skip trace them to get phone numbers for follow-up by cold call or SMS. Because the list is built from visible distress rather than a broad data pull, driving-for-dollars leads tend to be highly targeted, though the method is time-intensive to do at scale without apps or a team.
example: Driving a older neighborhood, you log 25 houses with tall grass, tarped roofs, or code notices. You skip trace the 25 owners, load the numbers into your dialer, and your cold caller works the list for motivated sellers.

faq.q: Is driving for dollars still worth it?
faq.a: Yes, especially for newer wholesalers with more time than budget. The leads are highly targeted because they are based on visible distress. The limitation is scale, which is why many operators combine it with larger skip-traced lists worked by cold callers.

faq.q: What happens after I log the addresses?
faq.a: You identify the owners, skip trace them for phone numbers, and add them to your outreach: cold calling and SMS. The driving stage builds the list; the conversations turn it into deals.

title: What Is a Cash Buyer in Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: A cash buyer purchases properties without financing and closes fast. Learn why wholesalers build cash buyer lists and how they drive disposition.
shortDefinition: A cash buyer is an investor who purchases properties without traditional financing and can close quickly. Cash buyers are the end buyers wholesalers assign or sell their contracts to.
longExplanation:
Cash buyers are the demand side of wholesaling. Because they do not wait on mortgage approval or appraisals, they can close in days, which is exactly what a wholesale assignment needs. Most cash buyers are fix-and-flip investors or buy-and-hold landlords looking for discounted, off-market properties.

Successful wholesalers build and maintain a cash buyer list well before they have a deal, so disposition is fast when a contract is signed. They track each buyer's buy box: preferred areas, property types, price ranges, and condition. When a deal matches a buyer's criteria, the wholesaler can move it in hours instead of scrambling, which is what makes a consistent assignment business possible.
example: You sign a contract Monday morning. Because you already know a flipper who buys 3/2 homes in that zip code under $160,000, you send it over, they confirm by afternoon, and the deal is assigned the same day.

faq.q: How do wholesalers build a cash buyer list?
faq.a: Through public records of recent cash purchases, local real estate investor meetups and REIA groups, online investor networks, and ads. The strongest lists come from buyers who have actually closed cash deals nearby in the last 12 months.

faq.q: Why are cash buyers important to wholesalers?
faq.a: They are the exit. A signed contract only becomes profit when an end buyer closes on it. Cash buyers close fast and reliably, which is what lets a wholesaler assign deals on a short timeline without financing risk.

title: What Is Proof of Funds (POF) in Real Estate? | VA Horizon
description: Proof of funds (POF) is a document showing a buyer has the cash to close. Learn how wholesalers and their cash buyers use POF in offers.
shortDefinition: Proof of funds (POF) is documentation, usually a bank statement or a lender letter, showing that a buyer has the cash available to complete a purchase. Sellers and agents often require it before accepting an offer.
longExplanation:
POF gives a cash offer credibility. When a wholesaler or their end buyer makes an offer without financing contingencies, the seller wants assurance the money is real. A proof-of-funds document, often a recent bank statement or a letter from a transactional or private lender, provides that assurance.

Wholesalers may present POF when making offers, particularly on agent-listed or bank-owned properties. Because the wholesaler often is not the party ultimately bringing the cash, they typically rely on their cash buyer's funds or a transactional lender's POF letter. Keeping a current POF ready speeds up offer acceptance and signals professionalism.
example: To submit an offer on an agent-listed property, you include a proof-of-funds letter from your transactional lender showing access to the purchase amount. The listing agent accepts the offer as a credible cash deal.

faq.q: Where do wholesalers get proof of funds?
faq.a: Commonly from their cash buyer's bank statements, from a private or transactional lender's letter, or from a partner funding the deal. The document just needs to credibly show the cash to close is available.

faq.q: Is proof of funds always required?
faq.a: Not on every direct-to-seller deal, but it is frequently requested on agent-listed and bank-owned properties. Having a current POF ready makes your offers more competitive and speeds acceptance.

title: What Is Absentee Owner in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Absentee Owner explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: An absentee owner owns a property they do not personally live in, often because it is rented, inherited, vacant, or held as an investment. Wholesalers target absentee owners because distance and deferred management can create selling motivation.
longExplanation: Absentee owner lists are popular because they combine ownership data with a practical reason for outreach. The owner may live across town or in another state, and the property may be harder for them to manage than a local home they occupy every day. A trained caller uses that context carefully, asking about the property, timeline, repairs, tenants, and whether the owner would consider a cash offer. The term does not mean the owner is distressed by default. It means the property is a reasonable starting point for a conversation, especially when layered with equity, age of ownership, vacancy, code issues, or other signals.
example: You pull a list of out-of-state owners with at least 40% equity, skip trace them, and have callers ask whether they would consider selling the rental they no longer want to manage.

faq.q: Why does Absentee Owner matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Absentee Owner matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

faq.q: Can a VA help with Absentee Owner?
faq.a: A trained VA can usually support the workflow around Absentee Owner: data cleanup, calling, CRM notes, follow-up tasks, buyer updates, and handoffs. Strategy, pricing, legal decisions, and final negotiations should stay with the business owner or licensed professional where required.

title: What Is Pre-Foreclosure in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Pre-Foreclosure explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Pre-foreclosure is the period after a borrower has fallen behind and received a default notice, but before the property is sold at foreclosure auction. It can signal urgency, but outreach must be handled with care.
longExplanation: Pre-foreclosure leads attract wholesalers because the owner may need a fast solution before the auction date. That urgency can create a real conversation, but it also raises the standard for professionalism. Callers should avoid pressure, explain the reason for the call plainly, and qualify whether the owner wants options rather than assuming distress. The best operators verify timelines, equity, liens, and payoff numbers before making promises. Many pre-foreclosure owners do not have enough equity for a wholesale deal, and some are already working with a lender or attorney.
example: A county record shows a default notice. Your caller confirms the owner wants to sell before auction, then the acquisitions team checks payoff, repairs, and ARV before making an offer.

faq.q: Why does Pre-Foreclosure matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Pre-Foreclosure matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

faq.q: Can a VA help with Pre-Foreclosure?
faq.a: A trained VA can usually support the workflow around Pre-Foreclosure: data cleanup, calling, CRM notes, follow-up tasks, buyer updates, and handoffs. Strategy, pricing, legal decisions, and final negotiations should stay with the business owner or licensed professional where required.

title: What Is Probate Lead in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Probate Lead explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A probate lead is a property connected to an estate after an owner has died. Heirs or personal representatives may consider selling if they do not want to maintain, repair, or divide the property.
longExplanation: Probate leads require tact. The owner of record may no longer be alive, and the person with authority to sell may be an executor, administrator, or heir. Wholesalers use probate records to identify estates with real property, then approach the contact with a calm, respectful tone. The opportunity comes from complexity: heirs may live out of state, disagree on repairs, or prefer a clean sale instead of listing the home. The deal must also clear title, court, and estate requirements, so patience and a good closing team matter.
example: An heir inherits a vacant house that needs a roof and cleanup. They do not want to coordinate repairs, so they consider a cash offer that can close after the estate paperwork is ready.

faq.q: Why does Probate Lead matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Probate Lead matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

faq.q: Can a VA help with Probate Lead?
faq.a: A trained VA can usually support the workflow around Probate Lead: data cleanup, calling, CRM notes, follow-up tasks, buyer updates, and handoffs. Strategy, pricing, legal decisions, and final negotiations should stay with the business owner or licensed professional where required.

title: What Is Tired Landlord in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Tired Landlord explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A tired landlord is a rental-property owner who may want to sell because of repairs, vacancies, tenant issues, management fatigue, or weaker cash flow.
longExplanation: Tired landlords are strong wholesaling prospects because their motivation is often operational, not theoretical. They may own a property that once looked like a good rental but now requires constant attention. Cold callers qualify these owners by asking about rent status, tenant cooperation, needed repairs, property management, and whether the owner would rather cash out than keep solving problems. The best lists combine landlord status with absentee ownership, long hold periods, code violations, eviction records, or older properties.
example: A landlord has a tenant behind on rent and a house needing $18,000 in repairs. A cash offer below retail may still be attractive because it removes the management headache.

faq.q: Why does Tired Landlord matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Tired Landlord matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

faq.q: Can a VA help with Tired Landlord?
faq.a: A trained VA can usually support the workflow around Tired Landlord: data cleanup, calling, CRM notes, follow-up tasks, buyer updates, and handoffs. Strategy, pricing, legal decisions, and final negotiations should stay with the business owner or licensed professional where required.

title: What Is High-Equity List in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: High-Equity List explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A high-equity list contains owners whose estimated property value is much higher than their mortgage balance. Equity gives a seller room to accept a discounted cash offer.
longExplanation: High equity does not automatically create motivation, but it makes a wholesale offer mathematically possible. If a seller owes nearly the full market value, there may be no spread after repairs and buyer profit. With strong equity, the acquisitions team has room to solve the seller problem and still leave margin for a cash buyer. Operators usually layer equity with better motivation signals such as absentee ownership, age of ownership, vacancy, probate, liens, tired landlord status, or pre-foreclosure.
example: You target owners with 50% or more equity who have owned the property for ten years. Callers qualify motivation before an acquisitions manager calculates the MAO.

faq.q: Why does High-Equity List matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: High-Equity List matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

faq.q: Can a VA help with High-Equity List?
faq.a: A trained VA can usually support the workflow around High-Equity List: data cleanup, calling, CRM notes, follow-up tasks, buyer updates, and handoffs. Strategy, pricing, legal decisions, and final negotiations should stay with the business owner or licensed professional where required.

title: What Is Distressed Property in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Distressed Property explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A distressed property shows physical, financial, legal, or ownership problems that may make a traditional retail sale difficult. Wholesalers look for distress because it can create discount opportunities.
longExplanation: Distress can mean visible repairs, tax issues, code violations, vacancy, title problems, foreclosure risk, or ownership complications. The important part is not the label; it is whether the problem creates a reason for the seller to prefer speed and certainty over a retail listing. Callers should avoid assuming a property is unwanted just because it looks rough. They should ask neutral questions, gather condition details, and route serious interest to acquisitions quickly.
example: A vacant house has boarded windows, old code notices, and an owner who lives three states away. Those signals justify outreach and a deeper qualification call.

faq.q: Why does Distressed Property matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Distressed Property matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

faq.q: Can a VA help with Distressed Property?
faq.a: A trained VA can usually support the workflow around Distressed Property: data cleanup, calling, CRM notes, follow-up tasks, buyer updates, and handoffs. Strategy, pricing, legal decisions, and final negotiations should stay with the business owner or licensed professional where required.

title: What Is Vacant Property in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Vacant Property explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A vacant property is not currently occupied. Vacancy can signal cost, risk, or neglect, making it a useful lead source for wholesalers.
longExplanation: Vacant properties can be expensive for owners to carry. Utilities, taxes, insurance, vandalism risk, code violations, and repairs continue even when nobody lives there. Wholesalers target vacancy because the owner may be more open to a simple cash sale, especially if the property is inherited, out of state, or difficult to rent. Good callers confirm whether the home is actually vacant, why it is vacant, and what the owner plans to do next.
example: A skip-traced vacant-house list gives your callers owners who may not want to keep paying taxes and maintenance on a property producing no rent.

faq.q: Why does Vacant Property matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Vacant Property matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

title: What Is List Stacking in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: List Stacking explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: List stacking is the practice of combining multiple lead lists and prioritizing properties that appear on several lists at once. More overlap usually means stronger motivation signals.
longExplanation: List stacking helps wholesalers move from broad outreach to smarter outreach. A property that is absentee-owned, high-equity, vacant, and code-violated is usually more interesting than a property with only one weak signal. Stacking also reduces wasted dialing by suppressing duplicates and ranking records before callers touch them. The goal is not to chase every possible list; it is to build a cleaner priority queue that improves conversations per hour.
example: Your CRM tags one property as absentee, vacant, and high equity. That stacked record gets called before a basic absentee-only lead.

faq.q: Why does List Stacking matter in wholesaling?

title: What Is Lead Qualifying in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Lead Qualifying explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Lead qualifying is the process of deciding whether a seller conversation has enough motivation, equity, condition detail, and timeline to become a real wholesale opportunity.
longExplanation: Qualifying protects the acquisitions team from chasing weak leads. A trained cold caller does not need to negotiate the whole deal, but they do need to capture the facts that make a lead worth follow-up: why the seller may sell, when they want to move, what repairs are needed, what price range they have in mind, and who has authority to decide. Clear qualification notes make speed-to-lead faster because the acquisitions manager can open the next call with context instead of starting over.
example: A caller tags a lead as hot because the seller wants to sell this month, knows the roof leaks, and is open to a cash offer. That lead goes straight to acquisitions.

title: What Is Seller Motivation in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Seller Motivation explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Seller motivation is the reason an owner may accept a cash offer below retail value. It is the difference between a conversation and a real wholesale lead.
longExplanation: Wholesaling depends on motivation because the offer must leave room for repairs, buyer profit, and the wholesale fee. Motivation can come from repairs, vacancy, probate, foreclosure risk, tenant problems, relocation, taxes, or simple fatigue. Callers uncover motivation by asking calm questions and listening for urgency, pain, and desired outcomes. A seller who only wants top-dollar retail pricing is not wrong; they are just not a wholesale fit.
example: A seller says they need the property sold before moving for a new job and do not want showings or repairs. That motivation may justify a fast cash offer.

faq.q: Why does Seller Motivation matter in wholesaling?

shortDefinition: A call disposition is the outcome label assigned after a call, such as no answer, wrong number, not interested, follow up, warm lead, or appointment set.
longExplanation: Call dispositions turn raw dialing into usable pipeline data. Without consistent labels, the team cannot tell whether a campaign is failing because of bad data, weak callers, poor timing, or low seller motivation. Good disposition rules are simple enough for callers to use quickly but specific enough for managers to coach from. They also power follow-up automations, so a warm seller gets a different next step than a disconnected number.
example: After each call, the VA chooses a disposition in the dialer. The CRM automatically creates a follow-up task for warm leads and suppresses wrong numbers.

Speed to lead depends on staffing, CRM routing, notifications, and clear ownership of follow-up. VA Horizon cares about this metric because callers create the opportunity, but the system must move it to acquisitions before the value leaks out.
example: A seller says yes to a cash-offer conversation at 10:14 a.m. The CRM alerts acquisitions instantly, and a follow-up call happens before the seller leaves lunch.

shortDefinition: A disposition manager sells or assigns contracted deals to cash buyers by matching each property to the right buyer list and managing the closing path.
longExplanation: Disposition is where a signed contract becomes collected revenue. The dispo manager packages the deal, sends it to qualified buyers, answers questions, coordinates showings or inspections, collects buyer feedback, and pushes the selected buyer toward closing. description: Buyer Buy Box explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A buyer buy box is the set of criteria a cash buyer uses to decide what deals they want, including location, price, property type, repairs, and exit strategy.
longExplanation: Tracking buy boxes makes disposition faster and more relevant. Instead of blasting every deal to every buyer, the team can match a contract to buyers who already said they want that type of property. Buy boxes usually include zip codes, beds and baths, maximum rehab level, price range, target ARV, rental versus flip strategy, and closing speed. Clean buy-box notes also help acquisitions know what to pursue in the first place.
example: A buyer wants 3/2 brick rentals under $140,000 in two suburbs. Your dispo manager sends only deals that match, improving response rates.

description: Buyers List explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A buyers list is a database of cash buyers and investors who may purchase wholesale deals. Quality matters more than raw list size.
longExplanation: A buyers list is only useful when it is segmented and current. Thousands of names do not help if nobody closes, nobody answers, or the team does not know each buyer criteria. Strong operators track who has bought recently, what areas they prefer, how they fund, what repair levels they accept, and how fast they can close. That information turns disposition into targeted matching instead of random email blasting.
example: Your list has 300 active buyers tagged by zip code and strategy. A new deal goes first to the 18 buyers who recently purchased nearby.

title: What Is Transactional Funding in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Transactional Funding explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Transactional funding is short-term capital used to complete the first leg of a double close, usually repaid when the resale closes shortly afterward.
longExplanation: Transactional funding helps wholesalers double close without tying up large amounts of their own cash. The lender funds the A-to-B purchase, then gets repaid from the B-to-C sale, often the same day. Because the money is short term and deal-specific, lenders want to see the end buyer contract, title coordination, and enough spread to support fees. It is not a substitute for a bad deal; it is a tool for structuring a good one.
example: You need to buy at $150,000 and resell at $165,000 the same day. A transactional lender funds the first close and is repaid from the second.

faq.q: Why does Transactional Funding matter in wholesaling?

description: Inspection Period explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: An inspection period is the contract window that allows the buyer to inspect the property and cancel under agreed terms before the deadline.
longExplanation: Inspection periods protect wholesalers while they confirm condition, repair scope, title, and buyer interest. A long inspection period gives more time to disposition the deal, but sellers may resist if they want certainty. A short period creates urgency for the team to move fast. The key is knowing the deadline and treating it seriously, because missing it can put earnest money at risk or weaken your negotiating position.
example: Your contract has a seven-day inspection period. The team gets photos, checks title, and sends the deal to buyers before day seven.

shortDefinition: Due diligence is the investigation a buyer performs before fully committing to a purchase, including condition, title, value, repairs, and exit strategy.
longExplanation: Due diligence is how a wholesaler confirms that a signed contract can actually close profitably. The team checks comps, repair estimates, liens, access, seller authority, title issues, tenant status, and buyer demand. Skipping diligence creates blown contracts, renegotiations, and reputation damage. Done well, it gives buyers confidence because the deal package is organized and realistic.
example: Before marketing a contract, the team verifies ownership, reviews photos, estimates repairs, and confirms that buyers in the area want that price point.

description: Clear Title explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Clear title means ownership can transfer without unresolved liens, claims, or defects that would block or cloud the sale.
longExplanation: A wholesale deal is not real until title can close. The title company researches those issues and tells the team what must be cleared. title: What Is 70 Percent Rule in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: 70 Percent Rule explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: The 70 percent rule is a quick investor formula that estimates a maximum offer as 70% of ARV minus repairs and the wholesaler fee.
longExplanation: The 70 percent rule is a screening tool, not a universal law. It helps wholesalers avoid overpaying by leaving room for the end buyer profit, closing costs, holding costs, and risk. Different markets may support higher or lower percentages depending on competition, repair uncertainty, and buyer appetite. The formula is most useful early in analysis; final offers should still reflect real comps, repairs, and what active buyers are willing to pay.
example: If ARV is $300,000 and repairs are $50,000, 70% of ARV is $210,000. After repairs and a $10,000 fee, the rough MAO is $150,000.

faq.q: Why does 70 Percent Rule matter in wholesaling?

title: What Is Repair Estimate in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Repair Estimate explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A repair estimate is the projected cost to bring a distressed property to the condition assumed in the ARV and buyer exit plan.
longExplanation: Repair estimates connect the property current condition to the finished value. If repairs are understated, the buyer margin disappears; if they are overstated, the seller offer may be too low to win. Wholesalers usually start with broad repair ranges, then improve accuracy with photos, walkthroughs, contractor input, and buyer feedback. The goal is not perfection; it is a defensible number that supports the MAO.
example: Photos show roof, flooring, kitchen, paint, and HVAC needs. The team estimates $45,000 in repairs before calculating the seller offer.

faq.q: Why does Repair Estimate matter in wholesaling?

title: What Is As-Is Value in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: As-Is Value explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: As-is value is what a property is worth in its current condition, before repairs or improvements are completed.
longExplanation: As-is value matters because sellers often compare your offer to what the property could sell for today without a full renovation. It is different from ARV, which assumes the property is repaired. Wholesalers use as-is value to understand seller expectations, investor risk, and whether a cash offer makes sense. In distressed properties, the as-is value may be far below retail because buyers must account for repairs, time, and uncertainty.
example: A renovated comp supports a $280,000 ARV, but the subject needs major work. Its as-is value may be closer to investor pricing than retail pricing.

faq.q: Why does As-Is Value matter in wholesaling?

description: Rehab Budget explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A rehab budget is the planned repair and renovation spend for a property, usually estimated before a cash buyer decides whether a deal works.
longExplanation: The rehab budget shapes the entire deal. Cash buyers compare purchase price plus repairs against ARV, financing costs, holding time, and target profit. If the budget is too low, the buyer gets surprised later; if it is too high, the deal may be rejected even when it could work. Wholesalers improve dispo by presenting realistic repair assumptions and clear photos instead of vague condition notes.
example: A buyer expects $55,000 in renovation costs. That number determines whether they can pay your asking price and still hit their profit target.

title: What Is Equity in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Equity explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Equity is the difference between a property estimated value and the debt owed against it. Higher equity gives more room for discounted offers.
longExplanation: Equity is one of the main filters behind wholesale lists. If a seller owes too much, even strong motivation may not leave enough room for repairs, buyer profit, and the wholesale fee. High equity does not guarantee a deal, but it increases the odds that a cash offer can solve the seller problem. Operators should still verify debt, liens, and payoff details before relying on an estimated equity number.
example: A property worth about $250,000 has a $90,000 mortgage balance. That equity may create room for a wholesale offer if the seller is motivated.

description: Comps Radius explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Comps radius is the geographic distance around a subject property used to select comparable sales for ARV analysis.
longExplanation: A tight comps radius usually gives a more reliable valuation because neighborhood differences can change value quickly. In dense urban areas, wholesalers may stay within a quarter mile. In suburban or rural markets, they may need to expand farther while paying attention to school zones, highways, property type, and buyer behavior. The radius is a starting point, not a substitute for judgment.
example: You start with sold renovated homes within half a mile. If there are not enough clean comps, you expand carefully while noting neighborhood differences.

title: What Is Wholesale Contract in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Wholesale Contract explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A wholesale contract is the purchase agreement between the seller and wholesaler that gives the wholesaler control of the deal before assignment or resale.
longExplanation: The contract is what turns a seller conversation into a controllable opportunity. It should clearly state the parties, property, price, closing date, earnest money, contingencies, and whether assignment is allowed. Wholesalers should use attorney-reviewed paperwork in their market because contract requirements and disclosure rules vary. A weak or unclear contract can make the deal hard to assign and harder to close.
example: A seller signs a purchase agreement with an assignability clause and inspection period. The wholesaler can now market the contract to buyers.

faq.q: Why does Wholesale Contract matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Wholesale Contract matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

faq.a: A trained VA can usually support the workflow around Wholesale Contract: data cleanup, calling, CRM notes, follow-up tasks, buyer updates, and handoffs. Strategy, pricing, legal decisions, and final negotiations should stay with the business owner or licensed professional where required.

title: What Is And/or Assigns in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: And/or Assigns explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: And/or assigns is contract language showing that the buyer may transfer their rights in the purchase agreement to another buyer.
longExplanation: The phrase is common in wholesaling because it supports assignment. It signals that the named buyer can assign the contract rights to an end buyer, often for a fee. The phrase alone is not a complete compliance strategy, though. Sellers should understand what assignment means, and some contracts or state rules may restrict it. Operators should confirm their paperwork with a local real estate attorney.
example: The buyer line says ABC Home Buyers LLC and/or assigns, making it clear the contract may be assigned before closing.

faq.q: Why does And/or Assigns matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: And/or Assigns matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

title: What Is Novation in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Novation explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A novation replaces an original contract with a new agreement, often allowing an investor to market the property differently than a simple assignment would.
longExplanation: Novation strategies are more complex than standard wholesale assignments. In a real estate novation, the seller may agree to let the investor market the property, coordinate improvements or listing activity, and be paid according to the new agreement. Because the structure affects control, disclosure, agency, and compensation, it requires careful paperwork and legal review. It is not a beginner shortcut; it is a specialized deal structure.
example: An investor signs a novation agreement allowing them to list the property and earn the spread between the seller payout and final buyer price.

faq.q: Why does Novation matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Novation matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

title: What Is Wholetail in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Wholetail explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Wholetail is a hybrid strategy where an investor buys a discounted property, makes light cleanup or repairs, and resells it quickly, often on-market.
longExplanation: Wholetailing sits between wholesaling and flipping. The investor actually takes title, improves presentation enough to widen the buyer pool, then resells without a full renovation. It can produce a larger spread than assignment, but it requires capital, closing ability, holding risk, and project discipline. Wholesalers may use wholetail when a property is too good to assign cheaply but not worth a full rehab.
example: You buy a dated house, clean it out, paint, replace carpet, and list it to investors and owner-occupants rather than assigning the contract.

faq.q: Why does Wholetail matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Wholetail matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

title: What Is Joint Venture (JV) in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Joint Venture (JV) explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A joint venture in wholesaling is a collaboration where two parties share roles and split the fee, often because one has the seller contract and the other has buyers.
longExplanation: JV deals can help wholesalers move contracts they cannot disposition alone. One partner may bring the deal, while the other brings buyers, funding, or closing expertise. Clear written terms matter: who controls communication, how the fee is split, who talks to title, and what happens if a buyer falls through. A messy JV can damage seller and buyer trust quickly.
example: You have a contract but no buyer in that market. A local wholesaler with a strong buyers list helps sell it, and you split the assignment fee.

faq.q: Why does Joint Venture (JV) matter in wholesaling?

title: What Is A2P 10DLC in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: A2P 10DLC explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: A2P 10DLC is the U.S. carrier registration system for application-to-person text messaging from local 10-digit numbers. Real estate SMS campaigns need it to reduce filtering risk.
longExplanation: A2P 10DLC matters because carriers now expect businesses sending texts from local numbers to register their brand and campaign use case. Wholesalers using SMS for seller outreach can face filtering, low deliverability, or platform restrictions if they ignore it. Registration does not make spam legal or guarantee delivery, but it is part of a compliant SMS setup alongside consent rules, opt-out handling, message quality, and list hygiene.
example: A wholesaler registers their brand and SMS campaign before sending follow-up texts from HighLevel, reducing carrier filtering compared with an unregistered number.

faq.q: Why does A2P 10DLC matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: A2P 10DLC matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.

title: What Is Do Not Call (DNC) in Real Estate Wholesaling? | VA Horizon
description: Do Not Call (DNC) explained for real estate wholesalers. Learn what it means, why it matters, and how it fits into seller outreach, offers, and deal flow.
shortDefinition: Do Not Call rules restrict certain telemarketing calls to numbers on federal or state DNC lists. Wholesalers running outbound campaigns need suppression and compliance processes.
longExplanation: DNC compliance is a serious operational requirement for cold calling and SMS teams. Wholesalers should suppress known DNC numbers, respect opt-outs, document consent where applicable, and avoid treating compliance as a dialer setting only. Rules can vary by campaign type, state, and relationship with the prospect, so operators should work with qualified counsel and use conservative processes. VA teams need clear scripts for opt-out requests and wrong-party calls.
example: Before a calling campaign starts, the list is scrubbed against DNC sources and callers are trained to mark any opt-out immediately in the CRM.

faq.q: Why does Do Not Call (DNC) matter in wholesaling?
faq.a: Do Not Call (DNC) matters because it affects how a wholesaling team finds sellers, qualifies motivation, prices offers, or moves contracts to closing. Clear definitions keep callers, lead managers, acquisitions, and disposition working from the same playbook.
